weekly_brief_draft:
## Lead Signal

This week the monitor records a structural reporting gap rather than a change in foreign information manipulation and interference activity. Across the May 9 to 15 window, the Interpreter found no new platform coordinated inauthentic behaviour disclosures, government attributions, or major research publications that meet the Tier 1 to Tier 3 threshold. The top level picture is therefore one of evidentiary absence, not operational pause. Russia, China, and Iran remain assessed as highly active or active based on existing baselines, but there is no fresh, date stamped evidence that would justify an update to those assessments in this cycle.

The lead signal is anchored in the persistence of earlier disclosures. Meta s H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report, published on March 11, remains the most recent comprehensive Tier 1 view of coordinated inauthentic behaviour networks across Meta surfaces. Google Threat Analysis Group s Q4 2025 bulletin from January 29 remains the latest detailed public account of cross platform influence operations from that source. No new Meta CIB reports, Google TAG bulletins, Microsoft MSTIC or MTAC influence operation posts, EEAS EUvsDisinfo alerts, or major civil society investigations were dated within the current week. Under the monitor s methodology this combination of factors is classified as a null signal week, but the underlying key judgment is that the gap is structural and driven by disclosure cadence.

## Other Developments

The absence of new campaign level disclosures leaves the actor tracker largely unchanged, but it also highlights the asymmetries in how different actors are documented. Russia remains listed as active, with a posture characterised by persistent multi platform coordinated inauthentic behaviour operations that target narratives in the post Soviet periphery, Western Europe, and Ukraine. The actor baseline notes an increasing use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts and confirms that the Portal Kombat domain network remains active across Google surfaces. There were no new disruptions or attributions concerning Russian operations this week, and the doctrine note explicitly states that posture is unchanged from the Meta H1 2026 and Google TAG Q4 2025 disclosures.

China is also assessed as active with a continuing high volume spam and pivot model often described under the Spamouflage or Taizi Flood labels. The baseline posture involves mass account acquisition and a flow of bland lifestyle content that serves as cover, with a small subset of assets pivoting to political narratives around United States China relations, Tibet, and Taiwan. The Meta H1 2026 report recorded increasing sophistication in these operations, but there were no additional platform disclosures in the current window. As with Russia, the doctrine note emphasises that there were no new doctrine developments this week and that the baseline posture remains aligned with the March Meta report.

Iran likewise retains an active status driven by cyber enabled influence operations associated with IUVM and the cluster that Meta described as Cotton Sandstorm. In the baseline, Meta s H1 2026 report updated the attribution of the long running Endless Mayfly operation to IUVM, and recorded the disruption of a new English language operation that targeted Western audiences but was taken down before it could build a substantial audience in Q4 2025. The Interpreter reiterates that the EU designation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps as a terrorist entity since 2023 has not visibly degraded operational tempo in the open sources used here. No new evidence of operational degradation or circumvention is recorded this week, reinforcing the judgment that risk from this actor remains elevated but stable.

For Gulf state, United States origin, and Israeli origin operations the week reinforces an attribution gap rather than a change in activity. The actor tracker describes Gulf state operations as structurally underrepresented in Tier 1 to Tier 3 disclosures, citing commercial relationships with Western platforms and limited civil society monitoring capacity as factors and recording that no new disclosures were found this week. United States origin operations are likewise structurally underreported, with domestic legal constraints, platform reluctance to designate United States origin coordinated inauthentic behaviour, and a reduced public output posture from the Global Engagement Center in 2025 to 2026 all cited as reasons. Israeli origin operations are described in similar structural terms, with explicit reference to European institutional perimeter exclusions and the European Parliament PEGA Committee s documentation of operations that met DISARM tactics, techniques, and procedures criteria but were not entered into the EEAS FIMI Explorer. In all three cases, this week brings no new disclosures and therefore no change to the baseline that lower confidence is structurally correct rather than an analytical failure.

Platform responses and transparency patterns remain a central driver of this null signal week. X, formerly Twitter, continues to operate without a coordinated inauthentic behaviour equivalent disclosure mechanism, leaving a structural gap with no weekly transparency reporting and a growing span of time without systematic takedown data. Meta has not published a new CIB report since the H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report on March 11, and the next expected disclosure is the H2 2026 report, estimated around September. Google and YouTube have not released a new TAG bulletin since the Q4 2025 instalment dated January 29, and the Q1 2026 bulletin that would normally be expected in April or May is now overdue. Microsoft has not published a new MSTIC or MTAC influence operations post within the May 9 to 15 window. TikTok is assessed as having accepted Digital Services Act commitments and typically publishes biannual transparency reports, but there was no new report this week. Telegram continues to provide no transparency reporting at all, preserving a separate structural gap.

These dynamics are captured directly in the risk indicator set. The platform opacity vector remains at an elevated level, with the summary noting that Meta s March report and Google TAG s January bulletin are still the most recent Tier 1 disclosures. The detailed analysis emphasises that Meta s biannual reporting, Google TAG s quarterly bulletins, and Microsoft s ad hoc posts create multi week gaps in which no new Tier 1 attribution evidence is available, and notes that the current nine week interval since the Meta report and the fifteen week interval since the Google TAG bulletin are within or just beyond their usual cycles but still leave real time monitoring dependent on Tier 2 and Tier 3 sources. The same vector stresses that the lack of a coordinated inauthentic behaviour mechanism on X or Twitter represents a permanent structural gap.

The attribution gap vector is also rated elevated and draws out the systematic imbalance between the six actor parity that the methodology seeks and the evidentiary reality. Russia and China are tracked across EEAS, Meta, Google TAG, and Microsoft MSTIC, while Iran is tracked by Meta and Microsoft. By contrast Gulf state, United States, and Israeli operations are not systematically tracked by any Tier 1 source. The narrative notes that this is a structural feature of the monitoring ecosystem, not an analytical failure, and that lower confidence for these actors is therefore a correct artefact of the evidence base.

Other risk vectors remain in place with no new data. The narrative persistence vector is rated moderate and highlights that campaigns such as Doppelganger, Endless Mayfly, and Portal Kombat meet the threshold of coordinated messaging across at least three weeks, based on the Meta H1 2026 and Google TAG Q4 2025 disclosures, but that there was no new narrative persistence evidence this week. The amplification reach vector stays at a moderate level, noting the absence of new bot network measurements or coordinated amplification findings and restating the baseline pattern in which Russia and China operate high volume amplification networks, Iran runs smaller but persistent networks, and Gulf state, United States, and Israeli amplification reach remains underdocumented. The AI enabled FIMI vector also remains moderate, with no new disclosures concerning synthetic media or AI assisted operations, and a baseline in which Russia and China are recorded as using AI assisted content in specific operations while other actors capabilities remain underdocumented.

On the regulatory side, the week is defined by continuity rather than change. European Union Digital Services Act Article 40 FIMI provisions remain in force, and very large online platforms are still required to assess and mitigate FIMI risks in their biannual transparency reports, but there were no new enforcement actions, fines, or compliance reports published in the current window. The EEAS FIMI Toolbox and Rapid Alert System remain active, with the most recent annual FIMI threats report dated to the third quarter of 2025 and no new reports or rapid alert disclosures this week. The European Union designation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps as a terrorist organisation has been in place since 2023 and continues with no newly documented operational effects or circumvention in this cycle. The NATO StratCom Centre of Excellence is recorded as continuing FIMI research and doctrine development, again without new publications this week.

## Cross Monitor Connections

Because this is a null signal week in evidentiary terms, cross monitor connections are driven primarily by structural themes rather than new events. The key judgments and governance modules all converge on the conclusion that platform disclosure cadence makes real time foreign information manipulation and interference monitoring impossible under current models. This has direct implications for the European strategic autonomy monitor, where Digital Services Act enforcement capacity and the latency of very large online platform reporting shape Europe s ability to manage systemic information risks.

The documented attribution gap between Russia, China, and Iran on one side and Gulf state, United States, and Israeli operations on the other also matters for the democratic integrity monitor. In election contexts, an ecosystem in which three actors are heavily documented across multiple institutional sources while others are structurally underreported can lead to skewed public and regulatory attention, even when the underlying operational tempo might be more balanced than the open data suggests.

The persistence of Russia linked campaigns such as Doppelganger and Portal Kombat and Iran linked operations such as Endless Mayfly, even without new weekly disclosures, is relevant to the conflict escalation monitor. These campaigns target narratives around Ukraine, Western Europe, and Western policy more broadly and form part of the background information environment in which conventional and hybrid conflicts unfold. The information environment and amplification modules also speak to the macro monitor by highlighting the concentration of attribution capacity in Meta, Google TAG, and Microsoft MSTIC. This concentration creates a single point of failure for visibility into information operations that interact with economic coercion narratives.

Finally, the AI enabled FIMI risk vector and the capability watch framing in the AI infrastructure modules interface directly with the AI governance monitor. The current cycle reports no new AI generated content, synthetic media, or AI assisted FIMI disclosures, but it maintains the baseline assessment of AI assisted content production in Russian and Chinese operations. The associated methodological caveat that AI content alone is not attribution evidence, and that MF2 applies to AI FIMI findings, is a shared concern across governance focused monitors that are grappling with evidentiary standards.

## Outlook

The outlook for the coming weeks is shaped less by expected shifts in actor behaviour and more by the timing of institutional disclosures. On the platform side, the key triggers remain the overdue Google TAG Q1 2026 bulletin, any new Microsoft MSTIC or MTAC influence operations posts, and the next tranche of Meta adversarial threat reporting, currently estimated for the second half of 2026. For regulatory enforcement, the critical developments would be the next round of very large online platform transparency reports under Digital Services Act Article 40 FIMI provisions and any new European Commission enforcement actions that clarify how those obligations are being applied in practice.

In the absence of those triggers, the monitor expects structural patterns to dominate. Platform opacity, attribution gaps between heavily documented and underdocumented actors, and concentrated attribution capacity will continue to constrain real time insight into foreign information manipulation and interference. The information integrity composite remains stable at its current score, reflecting medium strength attribution and transparency combined with weaker enforcement capacity and cross actor parity. Movement in that composite in future cycles is likely to be driven by changes in disclosure cadence and enforcement practice rather than by marginal shifts in individual campaigns, and the monitor will therefore continue to treat null signal weeks as meaningful indicators of systemic blind spots rather than as evidence of reduced adversary activity.
